data.title: BofA, JPMorgan, Evercore see Fed stopping balance sheet runoff soon
data.description:
The Federal Reserve could signal the end of its years-long process of shrinking its balance sheet, also called quantitative tightening or QT, as soon as next week, at least two Wall Street banks suggest. It all depends on whether the central bankers see liquidity in the economy as slightly above "ample."

Funding markets are experiencing "greater frictions," as illustrated by nearly zero overnight reverse repurchase facilities balances, strategists at J.P. Morgan observed in a note to clients. 

"Money markets at current or higher levels should signal to the Fed that reserves are no longer 'abundant'. By some metrics, the Fed many also judge that reserves are no longer 'ample,'" Bank of America strategists Mark Cabana and Katie Craig wrote in a note to clients. They now expect QT to stop at the end of October, compared with its prior expectation of at the end of December.

The U.S.'s central bank uses its balance sheet as a secondary tool to influence interest rates. Its primary tool is the federal funds rate target range, which controls short-term interest rates. During the pandemic, when rates were at nearly zero, the Fed bought U.S. Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities, holding them on its balance sheet as it injected cash into the economy to stimulate demand, a process called quantitative easing, or QE.

When inflation spiked, the Fed slowed then stopped its purchases of securities, then started letting the Treasuries and MBSs roll off its balance sheet when they matured. QT started in 2022, after its balance sheet swelled to $8.9T at March 30, 2022.  Three years later, it came down to ~$6.7T.

The J.P. Morgan strategists expect the shrinking to stop when the balance sheet reaches $6.6T.

"The Fed’s low tolerance for rate volatility and political pressures make another case for an earlier end to QT, as the Fed aims to avoid repeating September 2019 and maintain focus on its core mandate," the J.P. Morgan team, led by Teresa Ho, wrote.

Evercore ISI strategists, headed by Marco Casiraghi, expect the Fed will signal at next week's meeting the end of QT, which may actually end a month or two later. "The combination of higher money market rates and SRF (standing repo facility) usage suggests that aggregate reserves may be close to their minimum ample level," they wrote in a note issued earlier this week.

Earlier this month, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell started priming markets to expect such a change. He said the balance sheet runoff may end in "coming months" when reserves are somewhat above the level that the central bank considers "ample." Fed Governor Christopher Waller has also indicated he expects QT to end sooner rather than later.

As for the Fed's primary monetary policy tool, financial markets expect the Fed to cut its benchmark interest rate by another 25 basis points next Wednesday, bringing the federal funds rate target range to 3.75%-4.00%. Trading activity indicates a 98.9% probability of a 25-bp rate cut, according to the CME FedWatch tool. That compares with the recent cycle's peak of 5.25%-5.50% in August 2023 through August 2024.

data.title: First BanCorp signals 3–4% loan growth target for 2025 amid commercial and residential lending expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First BanCorp. (FBP) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Aurelio Alemán-Bermúdez, President, CEO & Director, opened the call highlighting, "We delivered another exceptional quarter of financial results that underscore our ability to produce consistent returns to our shareholders and consistent progress in our franchise metrics." Net income reached $100 million for the quarter, including the benefit of certain nonrecurring special items, with adjusted earnings per share up 13% year-over-year. He emphasized record net interest income, disciplined loan production, and a well-managed expense base.
  • Total loans grew by $181 million, marking 5.6% linked quarter annualized growth and surpassing $13 billion in total loans for the first time since 2010. Alemán-Bermúdez noted a slowdown in consumer credit demand, particularly in auto lending, influenced by sector-specific tariffs, with retail sales down 7% year-to-date and 17% lower in the third quarter compared to the prior year. The company mitigated this with growth in commercial and construction lending and stable residential mortgage production.
  • Core franchise deposits increased by $140 million, with Alemán-Bermúdez stating, "Trends in the market flows remain favorable. Although we're seeing higher competition in just seeking flows, we believe that could be temporary, particularly from affluent customers and government relations."
  • Asset quality remained stable, with a 7% reduction in nonperforming assets. The company repurchased $50 million in shares and announced a new $200 million share buyback program to be executed through 2026.
  • Orlando Berges-González, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Net income reaching $100 million or $0.63 a share. That compares to $80 million or $0.50 a share in the second quarter. Return on average assets for the quarter was 2.1%, much higher than last quarter." Berges-González detailed a $16.6 million reversal of valuation allowance on deferred tax assets, a $2.3 million collection of payroll taxes, and a $2.8 million valuation allowance for commercial other real estate property. After adjustments, non-GAAP adjusted earnings per share were $0.51 and return on average assets was 1.7%.

Outlook

  • Alemán-Bermúdez provided updated guidance: "Our loan growth guide for the year will probably be closer to the 3%, 4% range, depending on commercial credit line uses and any level of unexpected payments that we don't have knowledge today. We will provide an updated guide on our -- for 2026, once we report our fourth quarter in January, and also full year forecast for next year."
  • Loan originations excluding credit card activities are up 7% year-to-date compared to the prior year. Full-year guidance was revised down from mid-single-digit to the 3–4% range, citing normalization in auto lending and continued commercial and mortgage strength.

Financial Results

  • Reported net interest income for the quarter was $217.9 million, a $2 million increase from the previous quarter, including a $1.3 million improvement due to an extra day. Net interest margin reached 4.57%, up 1 basis point from last quarter, and 8% higher than the third quarter of the prior year.
  • Expenses were $124.9 million, $1.6 million higher than last quarter, mostly due to a $2.8 million valuation adjustment on a Virgin Islands property. Excluding certain items, expenses were $126.2 million, aligning with guidance of $125–126 million for upcoming quarters. The efficiency ratio was 50%.
  • The allowance for credit losses to loans decreased 4 basis points to 1.89%. Net charge-offs were $19.9 million, 62 basis points of average loans, up $800,000 from the prior quarter.
  • The tangible book value per share rose to $11.79, and the tangible common equity ratio expanded to 9.7%.

Q&A

  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde Group: Asked if the tax situation benefit is one-time. Orlando Berges-González replied, "There is a benefit on the normal operating losses or expenses we have at the holding company...not at the level of this reversal of DTA, but there is a little benefit on the effective tax rate going forward."
  • Rabatin asked about consumer health in Puerto Rico. Alemán-Bermúdez responded, "We expect stability on the consumer, but we don't expect portfolio growth as we achieved for some years. So the portfolio growth will come from resi, which is performing excellent and from the commercial portfolio that we continue to gain some share across the different sectors."
  • Rabatin questioned margin guidance with rate cuts. Alemán-Bermúdez noted, "We have some index deposits for the government that are -- they move with the rates, and some of that will come down. We don't see the other core retail products coming down yet."
  • Timur Braziler, Wells Fargo: Asked about competitive deposit pressures on the government side and expectations for deposit costs. Alemán-Bermúdez replied, "I think competitive pressures are really coming from the smaller players, not from the large players...We do expect some reduction in deposit costs coming down from -- as a result of the reduction in rates."
  • Braziler inquired about credit trends at other banks influencing reserving. Alemán-Bermúdez said, "We have a firm risk appetite and we have policies that we follow, and we have ticket -- deal size tickets that we have."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about new market entrants in Puerto Rico. Alemán-Bermúdez responded, "None of the deposits. It's really -- while we see, it's more aggressive now with the smaller players, as I mentioned."
  • Motta followed up on impacts from onshoring in Puerto Rico. Alemán-Bermúdez answered, "In the short term, they have announced a few deals ... But not necessarily, we see anything that must flow through the economy other than that impact in the short term."
  • Motta also asked about new loan origination yields. Berges-González replied, "The commercial portfolio yields are right now overall commercial portfolios, including everything, it's about [ 6.70% ] on average."
  • An unknown analyst asked about loan growth outlook into Q4. Alemán-Bermúdez reaffirmed, "The guidance that we have for the full year is between 3% and 4%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed neutral to slightly cautious sentiment, probing tax benefits, consumer credit health, and margin outlook amid competitive deposit pressures and rate cut expectations.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone, emphasizing "our ability to produce consistent returns" and "very proud of our team's accomplishments." Q&A responses were measured and focused on stability and risk management.
  • The tone was more cautious versus the previous quarter, especially regarding consumer lending and deposit competition, with a shift in guidance language from mid-single-digit loan growth to 3–4%.
  • Analysts' questions were more focused on risk and competitive pressures, whereas previously they probed growth and efficiency.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for loan growth was revised down from mid-single-digit to 3–4%, citing normalization in auto lending and more challenging consumer credit demand.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward commercial and residential mortgage lending, mitigating slower consumer segment performance.
  • Management's tone shifted from confident growth to a more cautious, stability-focused approach, particularly on loan growth and deposit competition.
  • Analysts' concerns moved from deposit stability and funding costs in Q2 to margin pressures, competitive dynamics, and sustainability of tax and credit trends in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited slowdown in consumer credit demand, especially auto lending, due to sector-specific tariffs and industry-wide sales declines.
  • Competitive pressures for deposits, particularly from smaller players and affluent customers, are expected to be temporary but closely monitored.
  • Exposure to government-related deposit flows and potential impacts from macroeconomic uncertainty, including federal government shutdowns and tariff-related inflationary pressures, were noted.
  • Ongoing litigation in the Virgin Islands resulted in a $2.8 million valuation allowance for commercial real estate property.
  • Credit remains stable, but management continues to watch for industry-wide impacts and evolving macroeconomic conditions.

Final Takeaway

Management highlighted another quarter of strong financial performance, with net income reaching $100 million and continued progress in loan and deposit growth, despite sector-specific headwinds in consumer lending. The company is executing on its strategy to drive growth through commercial and residential lending, returning capital to shareholders with a new $200 million buyback authorization, and maintaining a disciplined approach to risk and expense management. Forward guidance was revised to a 3–4% loan growth target for 2025, reflecting normalization in auto lending and ongoing strength in commercial and mortgage segments, while management remains focused on navigating competitive and macroeconomic challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IMAX projects full-year installations at high end of 150–160 range as global box office surges 50%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: IMAX Corporation (IMAX) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Richard L. Gelfond highlighted, "We delivered our highest third quarter revenue ever with $106.7 million and our best ever quarterly cash flow with $67.5 million. We drove growth of more than 30% across gross margin, net income, adjusted EBITDA and earnings per share. Our third quarter earnings exceeded those of our first and second quarters combined. The third quarter of 2025 was our highest grossing Q3 ever at the global box office with $368 million, up 50% year-over-year."
  • Gelfond stated that signings of new and upgraded IMAX systems reached 142 through September, surpassing the full year total for 2024. He projected reaching the high end of the guidance range for installations, "We now expect to hit the high end of our guidance of between 150 and 160 installations for the full year."
  • The CEO emphasized IMAX's diversified content portfolio as a growth driver, noting success with both Hollywood and local language titles, and highlighted that international films now account for 36% of global box office year-to-date, up from less than 20% last year.
  • CFO Natasha Fernandes reported, "Third quarter IMAX box office of $368 million was 50% higher year-over-year and exceeded Street estimates by more than 25%. Signings for IMAX systems at the end of September was 142, already eclipsing full year 2024, and system installations are now tracking to the high end of our guidance range of 150 to 160 systems."

Outlook

  • Gelfond noted optimism about reaching the $1.2 billion global box office guidance for the year. He said, "We're now very well positioned to deliver on that guidance."
  • Fernandes stated, "Our strong operational performance led to a third quarter total consolidated adjusted EBITDA of $52 million, which increased $13 million or 34% year-over-year, driven by higher revenues, which mostly flow through to gross margin."
  • Management reiterated plans to provide further strategic direction during the upcoming Investor Day in December.

Financial Results

  • IMAX reported revenues of $107 million in Q3, a 17% increase over the prior year third quarter of $91.5 million. Gross margin rose to $67 million, reflecting a 32% year-over-year increase and a margin of 63%, which is a 740 basis point improvement over the prior year period.
  • Content Solutions revenues reached $45 million, up 49% year-over-year, with a gross margin of 71% compared to 55% in the prior year. Adjusted EPS was $0.47, up $0.12 year-over-year.
  • Cash flow from operations was $67.5 million, setting a new quarterly record. Year-to-date cash flow from operations was $98 million, already surpassing the 2024 full year operating cash flow of $71 million by 40%.
  • The company reported a Q3 ending cash balance of $143 million. Debt, excluding deferred financing costs, was $261 million, and available liquidity was approximately $544 million.

Q&A

  • Eric Handler, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC: Asked about margin potential and cost stability in Content Solutions. Fernandes responded that Q3's 71% margin was a high and that as box office climbs, incrementality flows through at an 85% rate, with costs remaining stable due to efficiency measures and limited increases in SG&A.
  • Handler followed up on exhibitor demand and the volume of requests for IMAX systems. Gelfond replied, "There are a lot of activity going on around the world... we've never really had a backlog of films going that far forward."
  • Eric Wold, Texas Capital Securities, Research Division: Inquired about capacity constraints and ticket pricing power. Gelfond said there is "room to fit more content" and noted that ticket price increases are likely to be driven by exhibitor decisions, especially with a strong film slate in 2026.
  • Andrew Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc.: Asked about 2026 box office growth and major tentpoles. Gelfond indicated confidence in the slate exceeding comps, with diversification across five significant films.
  • Mike Hickey, The Benchmark Company, LLC: Asked about rest of world market share and installation growth. Gelfond stated, "The only thing holding us back... is more theaters" in underpenetrated markets, and clarified there is "no pull forward" on installations, with strong momentum expected to continue.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally positive, frequently congratulating management on record results, though questions sought clarity on margin sustainability, cost controls, and future growth levers. There was interest in the ability to maintain high margins, expand internationally, and further monetize alternative content.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, emphasizing record-setting performance, strong backlog, and growth opportunities. Fernandes exhibited confidence in margin expansion and operational efficiency. Gelfond’s tone was assertive during both prepared remarks and Q&A, especially when addressing installation growth and competitive positioning.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone from both analysts and management was more assertive and upbeat, reflecting increased confidence after exceeding prior guidance and establishing new records.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year installations remained at 150–160, but management now expects to reach the high end of this range after surpassing prior year signings in just nine months.
  • Revenue, gross margin, cash flow, and adjusted EPS all increased sequentially and year-over-year, with Q3 cash flow from operations substantially higher than in Q2.
  • Management's tone shifted from cautiously optimistic in Q2 to confident in Q3, repeatedly highlighting record achievements and a strong slate for 2026 and beyond.
  • Analysts’ focus evolved from questioning the sustainability of installation and box office growth to probing opportunities for further margin expansion and international market penetration.
  • Strategic priorities remain centered on expanding the global network, deepening content diversification, and leveraging operational leverage for incremental profits.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential for increased costs related to marketing major titles such as Avatar, which could impact Q4 EBITDA margin.
  • Geographic expansion is partly constrained by the pace of securing new theater locations, particularly in underpenetrated regions like Southeast Asia and India.
  • Analysts raised questions about the sustainability of high margins and the timing of installation growth, as well as the ability to maintain box office momentum against tough comps in future quarters.
  • Management flagged the importance of content mix and timing, especially regarding alternative content and the allocation of marketing resources.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that IMAX delivered record-setting financial and operating results in the third quarter, surpassing previous benchmarks for revenue, cash flow, and system signings. With a strong and diversified content slate, robust global network expansion, and increased market share, the company sees itself as well positioned to meet or exceed full-year guidance and drive further growth into 2026 and beyond, supported by a healthy pipeline of installations and blockbuster releases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Covenant Logistics signals further margin compression and capacity exits as regulatory changes accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Covenant Logistics Group, Inc. (CVLG) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Parker reported that "our business remained resilient in the third quarter, although margins were compressed, particularly in our Asset-Based Truckload segment due to an inflationary cost environment, persistently high claims expense, headwinds from excessive unproductive equipment and continued pressure on volume and yields in our Expedited and Dedicated segments."
  • Parker highlighted that consolidated freight revenue increased by $10.2 million to $268.9 million, while consolidated adjusted operating income shrank by 22.5% to $15 million, citing increases within the Truckload segment.
  • The company’s net indebtedness as of September 30th increased by $48.6 million to $268.3 million, attributed to share repurchases and acquisition-related earn-out payments.
  • Grant reported, "Expedited segment yielded a 93.6% adjusted operating ratio...the average fleet size shrunk by 31 units or 3.4% to 861 average tractors in the period."
  • Parker stated, "we were successful in growing the dedicated fleet by 136 tractors or approximately 9.6% compared to the prior year as we have continued to win new business in specialized and high service niches."
  • Managed Freight segment saw sequential declines after losing a short-term customer but outperformed the prior year. Warehouse segment posted sequential improvement with an adjusted operating ratio of 92.1% and expects revenue and income growth from a major customer start-up in November.
  • Parker warned, "we anticipate the fourth quarter of the year to remain challenging, with the continuation of the soft freight market, combined with the impact of company-specific factors that will result in what we believe to be an unseasonably soft quarter."
  • CFO James Grant stated, "there's going to be some pain before there's some gain and pain in used truck prices and [see] smaller guys go bankrupt and flood the market, pain with some brokerage compression. But every time in history in this business, there has to be pain before there's gain."

Outlook

  • Management anticipates a challenging Q4, citing a soft freight market, increased claims accruals, government shutdown impacts on Department of Defense freight, and accelerated customer bankruptcies in TEL.
  • The company expects that "cost to procure transportation will likely lead our ability to capture rate increases from our customers in our Managed Freight segment, resulting in constrained margins."
  • Parker expressed optimism about the medium-term, stating, "we are increasingly optimistic about the pace at which the freight market should recover."

Financial Results

  • Consolidated freight revenue increased by $10.2 million to $268.9 million.
  • Adjusted operating income decreased by 22.5% to $15 million.
  • Net indebtedness increased by $48.6 million to $268.3 million.
  • Expedited segment’s operating ratio was 93.6%, with a fleet size of 861 average tractors.
  • Dedicated segment’s operating ratio was 94.7%, with the fleet growing by 136 tractors.
  • Managed Freight revenue and operating income exceeded the prior year but declined sequentially due to a short-term customer loss.
  • TEL contributed pretax net income of $3.6 million for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Asked about market capacity exits and spot rates. Parker responded that regulatory crackdowns are tightening supply, particularly regarding non-domicile drivers and ELD compliance, and expects fewer new entrants: "there is actually a constraining of supply that's happening."
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Queried margin compression risks for brokerage. Parker confirmed concern but expects asset rates to rise over time, offsetting brokerage margin pressure.
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Asked for sizing of Q4 impact and LTL performance. Parker noted LTL is down and expects Q4 to be "less than third quarter."
  • Jason Seidl, TD Cowen: Inquired about DoD business and government shutdown effects. President M. Bunn explained, "about half that business will kind of just be lost...if it lasts the whole quarter, it's going to be pretty impactful on Expedited's results."
  • Seidl also questioned capacity and insurance. Parker expected insurance companies will "not insure non-domiciled CDL license" and sees government and insurance pressure leading to capacity exits.
  • Reed Seay, Stephens Inc.: Asked about cost management. Bunn explained continued cost controls, matching headcount to volumes, and managing maintenance costs.
  • Jeffrey Kauffman, Vertical Research Partners: Asked about share repurchases. Parker acknowledged shares are "highly discounted" and noted availability under the repurchase program but made no commitments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on downside risks, regulatory impacts, and cost pressures, with a slightly negative tone and focus on near-term uncertainties.
  • Management was defensive in addressing margin pressures and regulatory headwinds but expressed strong medium-term optimism, with Parker stating, "I'm more excited right now than I've ever been in my entire career for the next 2 to 3 years."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism increased while management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to a mix of near-term realism and longer-term bullishness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language turned more cautious, with management calling Q4 "unseasonably soft," in contrast to prior expectations for sequential improvement.
  • Strategic focus shifted from fleet and revenue growth to margin management, cost controls, and navigating regulatory changes.
  • Key metrics showed consolidated revenue declined from Q2, margins remained under pressure, and debt increased.
  • Analyst focus moved from growth opportunities to risk management and downside protection.
  • Management confidence in medium-term market recovery became more emphatic, while near-term caution increased.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited regulatory crackdowns on non-domicile drivers, ELD enforcement, and potential insurance restrictions as major industry challenges.
  • Internal headwinds include persistent claims expense, inflationary cost environment, excess unproductive equipment, and customer bankruptcies in TEL.
  • The government shutdown’s impact on DoD freight is expected to be "pretty impactful" if prolonged.
  • Cost controls and business mix realignment are ongoing mitigation efforts, as described by Bunn and Grant.

Final Takeaway

Covenant Logistics management highlighted persistent margin pressure and regulatory headwinds as short-term challenges, with significant operational adjustments underway across segments. The company expects Q4 to be unusually soft but maintains high conviction in a freight market recovery driven by regulatory shifts reducing capacity and improving long-term fundamentals. Management is focused on disciplined cost controls and portfolio optimization to navigate the current environment and position for future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: P&G poised for a softer fiscal start amid efforts to boost global market growth
data.description:
Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG) is scheduled to announce first-quarter earnings results on October 24, before market open. Analysts at RBC Capital Markets expect the company to have a slow start to the fiscal year, with Q1 coming in toward the lower end of PG’s annual guidance.

The consensus EPS estimate is $1.90, which is a 1.6% rise compared to the same quarter last year, while the consensus revenue estimate is $22.17 billion, translating to a 2% year-on-year rise.  

Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 13 downward revisions while revenue estimates have seen 2 upward revisions and 7 downward moves.

Citing an industry conference in early September, RBC Capital Markets said it expects the company to have a steady quarter but noted that growth has remained slow since then.

Analysts led by Nik Modi said that the consumer goods giant currently sees global market growth stable in the 2-2.5% range, slower than in the past year but within expectations. North America is in the 2-3% range with signs of stabilisation, while they see Europe flattening out. In China, conditions remain difficult, though management said its strategies are “moving into a positive trajectory.”

Management described market growth, tariffs, commodities, and foreign exchange as “not great but stable,” adding that their top priority is to reignite global market growth, RBS additionally noted.

The brokerage sees the conglomerate reiterating its annual guidance, but is likely to remain towards the mid to low point of ranges.

“We see the US growth trajectory as the biggest swing factor with PG currently trending in the 1.5-2.5% range. Internationally (excluding China), we would expect modest (lower-single-digit to middle-single-digit percentage) growth with sequentially improving trends in China. PG’s global aggregate share trends have slowed, and we would expect continued reinvestment to support areas where share has softened,” RBC said.

Over the last 2 years, PG has beaten EPS estimates 100% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 38% of the time.
